So, I'll start by saying we are in a
risk-on market regime and have been in a
risk-on market regime since early April.
Uh that's being driven by three primary
factors. As we look at the six key macro
cycles, the monetary policy cycle still
in a meaningful tailwind as it relates
to asset markets. The fiscal policy
cycle is in a modest tailwind as it
relates to its impact on asset markets.
And the liquidity cycle is still in a
meaningful tailwind as it relates to its
impact on asset markets. And so
investors should expect that this risk
on market regime is likely to continue
until we see one or more of those
tailwinds devolve into headwinds. If if
you look forward one to two quarters,
we're concerned that the monetary policy
cycle could devolve into a headwind and
also take the liquidity cycle into a
headwind because we're expecting uh some
tightening monetary policy out of the
Federal Reserve.

think about core PCE it's annualizing at
3.5% 3-month annualized. Trim mean PCE
which is Fed chair Kevin Warship's
preferred measure for now is annualizing
at 2.7% through annualized. the the trim
mean PC is likely to continue to
gravitate higher in the coming months uh
based on our analysis. And so ultimately
where we think we're headed especially
as we look out into 2027 and beyond is a
Federal Reserve that is very doubbish
when you think about the likely outcome
from the five key task forces on a net
basis. It's going to send very doubbish
policy signals to the FOMC and they're
ultimately going to lower the policy
rate and in in a material degree
starting next year. And so if you go
from today's inflation problem to very
dovish monetary policy without any
tightening in between, you're going to
have a bigger inflation problem in the
future. And so ultimately, we think they
just need to create a little bit of
scope and regain some of that

>> Yeah. So, uh thank you for highlighting
productivity. Uh because ultimately
you're you're spotlighting what I think
is the most important dynamic right now
is the sequence of the AI capex and its
relationship to the economy. Right now
we're spending and the money's going out
the door and that is inflationary. It's
a massive aggregate demand shock. It's
one of the largest aggregate demand
shocks we've ever seen in recorded human
history. It's about 2.5% of nominal GDP
here in 2020. uh a six in terms of the
$800 billion dollars at hyperscaler
capex that's currently projected next
year the number is currently projected
to be $1.2 2 trillion. That's about 3.5%
of nominal GDP in 2027. So this is this
is I mean enormous in terms of the
aggreate demand shock. So that is
pushing up growth. It's pushing up
inflation. On the other side of this, I
think you you would most most investors
would agree we're likely to wind up with
a structural uptrend in productivity
growth. I would argue that's already
started. Our data shows that that that
process has already started. And so
we're going to wind up with a structural
uptrend in productivity growth. And this
impacts financial markets through three
channels. one that should lower the
trend rate of core inflation by our
calculus. It's about minus 50 basis
points in trend inflation. So that's
great in terms of reflating market
multiples and taking pressure off the
Federal Reserve. And two, it should also
reflate the the the mean rate of change,
the trend rate of change, the ch trend
growth rate of corporate profits by
about 50% in terms of transitioning from
a 1 to 2% trend productivity economy to
a 3 to 4% trend productivity economy.
you tend to have the median growth rate
of nipple corporate profits accelerate
by about 50 basis points with data going
all the way back to 1948. So that's
positive as well. And then lastly, you
tend to see the S&P 500, the stock
market grow on a compounded growth rate
basis by about 50% faster when you make
that transition from 1 to 2% trend
productivity to 3 to 4% trend
productivity. So that's where we're
headed long term. But in the meantime,
we have to deal with the massive aggre
demand shock and the inflation that's
causing here the real economy. Now

We there the labor market's being
impacted on on two vectors, two pretty
material vectors. One, we have a
confluence of trailing monetary
stimulus, fiscal stimulus, and
deregulation that's pushing up nominal
growth in the economy. So, we have a
cyclical demand for labor. We can see
this in the Jolts data uh and and and
the trailing strength of the employment
growth. However, when you kind of go
underneath the hood, uh, and look at
things like the, you know, jolts uh, uh,
job openings, the hires rate spread, or
the the long-term unemployment rate or
or the unemployed jolts to unemployed
ratio, those types of ratios that tell
you what the true health of the labor
market is, they're they're going in a
different direction from the overall
kind of trend momentum in the jobs. And
so, when you look at the unemployment
rate, the headline unemployment rate of
4.2%, 2% that's modestly below NEU which
is the government's estimate of what you
know full employment is and so we have a
tight labor market that's squishing
underneath the surface that is being
propped up by the confluence of fiscal
monetary and and and regulatory easing.
Once we get out of 27 and 20 26 27 that
fiscal monetary and regulatory easing

we're likely to wind up with a much
